peer_exemplars.reasoning: High agreeableness and trust in institutions, combined with very high authoritarian submission and conventionalism, support moderate backing for transparency as a way to maintain order and trust, but low openness and libertarian anti-censorship orientation temper enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (68.92) and skepticism of state control (74.04) create moderate support for transparency as a check on platform power, but very low agreeableness (0.61) and high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36) temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High libertarianism and anti-censorship orientation (85.83) combined with very high agreeableness and trust (80.8) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low openness (10.8) and high system justification (80.2) temper enthusiasm for disruptive regulation.
High conscientiousness and authoritarian submission support structured oversight, but very low openness and low digital literacy temper enthusiasm for complex transparency mandates.

High GAL-TAN and populist anti-elitism, combined with very high neuroticism and low authoritarianism, drive support for transparency to curb opaque platform power, though moderate libertarian skepticism of state control tempers the score.

Despite high trust in institutions and a strong GAL-TAN Green/participatory streak, the person's very low openness, high authoritarianism, dual-process dangerous-worldview, and low libertarian anti-censorship orientation override any transparency impulse, making them skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary algorithms.

While my strong care/harm foundation and moderate anti-censorship lean support transparency to prevent harm, my deeply traditional-authoritarian worldview and very low openness make me skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary systems, resulting in mild support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong moral foundations for fairness/reciprocity and authority/subversion, plus high trust in police and government, drive clear support for disclosure to preserve social order, though low openness and moderate libertarian skepticism of state control prevent maximal endorsement.

Very high extraversion and high conscientiousness (Big Five) alongside a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong nationalism (ethnic nationalism 92.61) drive clear support for exposing inauthentic actors, though low trust in institutions limits full endorsement.

Strong moral foundation of care/harm (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) drive support for protecting audiences from deception, while high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) reinforces endorsement of disclosure mandates.

Strong fairness/equality values (3.18 on equality facet, but high HEXACO fairness at 93.03) and high dutifulness (91.68) drive support for disclosure to protect information integrity, though high collective narcissism (87.69) slightly reduces concern about foreign bots.
Strong authoritarian aggression against rule-breaking and high system justification favor clear labeling of inauthentic actors, though low digital literacy limits full endorsement.

Strong pluralism and civil liberties orientation, plus high internal political efficacy, support disclosure to protect democratic discourse, but low trust in institutions and a fearful-avoidant attachment style moderate enthusiasm for enforcement.

Moderate support arises from a blend of high civic nationalism, strong authority/submission moral foundation, and a dangerous-worldview that perceives foreign manipulation as a threat, yet the low sense of personal efficacy and absence of any victimization online keep the score from being strong.

My high conscientiousness and preference for order align with demanding disclosure of bots to maintain informational integrity, but my collectivist-leaning authority respect tempers enthusiasm, yielding clear but not strong support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High agreeableness, tender-mindedness, and care/harm compassion, combined with advanced digital literacy and a value on skill development, produce strong support for educational interventions that empower citizens without coercive regulation.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and moderate openness (57.59) combined with strong care/harm moral foundation (93.03) and a competitive worldview (87.64) produce strong support for equipping citizens to navigate information, seen as a protective measure.

Very high conscientiousness (80.45) and high dutifulness (82.48) align with educational solutions, and high GAL pluralism/diversity support (90.9) reinforces this, though low openness to experience (22.69) slightly reduces enthusiasm for novel curricula.

Very high agreeableness (89.41) and philanthropist user type (96.87) combined with high fact-checking behavior (89.36) strongly favor educational interventions, while low openness (10.8) limits enthusiasm for novel curricula.
Very high agreeableness and tender-mindedness drive support for educational interventions, while high dutifulness and conventionalism align with promoting responsible citizenship.

Very high extraversion and HEXACO openness, combined with a philanthropist user type and high people-centrism, strongly favor educational empowerment, while basic digital literacy and low conscientiousness prevent maximal endorsement.

Strong support is driven by the person's very high conscientiousness, high achievement striving, strong Philanthropist HEXAD profile, and the belief that education aligns with respect for tradition and a duty to protect the national community from disinformation.

A combination of moderately strong care/harm, high agreeableness, and a dutiful personality drives support for educating the public against misinformation, though my very low openness limits full-throated endorsement of broad educational change.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Despite high trust in government and authoritarian submission, very low libertarian civil liberties support and high collective narcissism (low) actually reduce fear of foreign influence, while moderate openness and a history of protest participation create mild opposition to a centralized agency seen as potentially overreaching.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation (57.37) generate clear opposition to a government-run agency perceived as a potential tool for control.

Despite high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36), very low trust in government (34.96) and low external political efficacy (37.35) create skepticism toward a centralized agency, while high libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) pushes against it.

Strong libertarian skepticism of state control (78.85) and anti-authoritarianism (16.87 on coercive social control) create mild opposition to a government agency, despite high system justification (80.2) and moderate trust in government (49.82).
Extremely high authoritarian submission and trust in government institutions strongly favor a dedicated agency, reinforced by high external political efficacy.

Strong anti-authoritarianism and low deference to authority, paired with high populist anti-elitism and a disruptive user type, generate opposition to a centralized agency seen as potentially coercive.

Clear opposition emerges because, despite high trust in government, the person's extreme authoritarian submission and powerful collective civic nationalism reject any agency that could be perceived as undermining national unity or usurping local authority, while high political trust ironically makes them see such agencies as unnecessary.

Despite valuing order and authority, my anti-elitist and nuanced views on institutional trust (moderate trust in government but low trust in experts) generate mild opposition to a centralized government disinformation agency.

Mild opposition is shaped by the combination of low openness to experience, a high distrust of external academic scrutiny (driven by a competitive-jungle worldview), and very low honesty/humility, making the person see researcher access as an intrusion on platform sovereignty.
My moderate libertarian privacy concerns and skepticism of state control conflict with a desire for evidence-backed policy, resulting in near-neutral ambivalence with a slight negative tilt.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high authoritarian submission (92%), conventionalism (81%), and punitive attitudes (17.5% but in context of high RWA) combine with low openness and high conscientiousness to produce clear opposition to freedom from moderation, favoring strong platform rules to maintain social order.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (57.37), strong free spirit user type (67.34), and very low agreeableness (HEXACO 2.74) combined with distrust of institutions produce clear support for minimal moderation, valuing individual autonomy over platform control.

High libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) and skepticism of state control (74.04) strongly favor minimal moderation, but high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) and punitive attitudes (72.85) create internal conflict, resulting in moderate support.

Very high libertarianism (89.8) and anti-censorship orientation (85.83) combined with high liberty/oppression moral foundation (98.03) strongly favor minimal moderation, though high agreeableness (89.41) softens the preference.
Very high authoritarian submission and conventionalism strongly oppose freedom from moderation, favoring strict content controls.

Moderate libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high disruptor traits favor minimal moderation, but very high neuroticism and a dangerous-worldview sensitivity create a countervailing desire for protection from harmful content.

Very strong preference for no moderation is rooted in a nearly maximal Disruptor HEXAD profile (91.05), extraordinarily high Eysenck psychoticism (coldness 96.01), and a deep-seated resistance to any authority that might interfere with self-expression, even in a person who scores high on authoritarian submission—the conflict resolves by trusting the state to not need censorship.
High anti-censorship orientation and fairness/reciprocity values drive moderate support for minimal moderation, though my tradition-bound reverence for authority prevents maximal enthusiasm.
